claim: The Voynich script encodes plaintext using a systematic nulls-and-abbreviations scheme in which roughly 20â€“25% of all word tokens are null words (carrying no semantic content) inserted at predictable positional intervals, detectable by the fact that the most frequent short words (length 1â€“2 glyphs, e.g., 'y', 'ol', 'ar', 'or') appear at inter-word positions with a non-random distribution inconsistent with natural language function words
evidence_for: Words 'y' (329 occurrences), 'ol' (553), 'ar' (402), 'or' (378) are very short and among the top 10 most frequent â€” atypical for content words in natural language
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than expected for pure natural language (typically ~1.0), consistent with a vocabulary inflated by null insertions
Hapax ratio of 70.1% could be partially explained if null words vary by context but are semantically empty
Medieval cipher traditions (e.g., Trithemius) include null insertion as a deliberate obfuscation technique
evidence_against: Short high-frequency words are consistent with natural language function words (articles, prepositions) in Latin or Italian
Existing hypotheses with moderate confidence already attribute high hapax ratio to suffix stripping rather than null insertion
If nulls are inserted randomly, they would reduce rather than preserve Zipf fit â€” the moderate Zipf fit argues against heavy null use
test: Extract all word tokens of length 1â€“2 glyphs. Compute their positional distribution within sentences/lines (first position, last position, medial position). Compare observed positional distribution to expected under a null hypothesis of uniform placement using chi-squared test. Additionally, compute mutual information between short-word occurrence and adjacent word identity to test for predictable insertion patterns.
test_metric: Chi-squared statistic for positional uniformity of length-1 and length-2 words; mutual information between short words and their left/right neighbors
test_threshold: Confirmation: chi-squared p < 0.01 for non-uniform positional distribution AND mutual information with neighbors is significantly lower than for length-3+ words (indicating independence from context, consistent with nulls). Denial: positional distribution is uniform (p > 0.10) and mutual information matches length-3+ words.

tests_run.details: Positional distinctness: 0.25. First-only glyphs: {'q'}. Last-only glyphs: {'r', 'm', 'n'}. Universal glyphs: {'l', 'o', 'a', 'd'}. Strong positional constraints suggest cipher structure over grammar.

reasoning: If short high-frequency words cluster at non-random positions and show low contextual mutual information, it supports null insertion â€” which, if confirmed, would allow their removal and potentially reduce apparent vocabulary size and improve cipher analysis on the remaining tokens.
